## Transfer payment programs of $5 million or more

### 9-8-8: Suicide Crisis Helpline
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19 and 2023–24
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
This program supports the implementation and operation of the 9-8-8: Suicide Crisis Helpline , a three-digit number for accessing suicide prevention support via voice and text. Three years of funding for 9-8-8 was announced in Budget 2023 and will end in March 2026. PHAC is undertaking a review to establish a long-term funding mechanism for 9-8-8 post March 2026.
The objective of the 9-8-8: Suicide Crisis Helpline is to provide people across Canada with an easy-to-remember number to access live bilingual crisis support 24 hours a day, seven days a week, from trained responders using the modality of their choice, voice or text. Access to 9-8-8 services in other languages may be available by phone via an interpreter for non-English or non-French speaking Canadians.
The Centre for Addiction and Mental Health (CAMH) leads the coordination of service delivery for 9-8-8. Calls and texts to 9-8-8 are answered by trained responders at one of about 40 local, provincial, territorial, and national crisis lines who are part of the 9-8-8 network of partners. Where possible, callers and texters are routed to the nearest available responder. A national back-up hub of trained responders provides additional capacity if a local responder is not available. 9-8-8 network partners provide existing crisis services within their communities, and also answer 9-8-8 calls and texts.
9-8-8 builds on Talk Suicide Canada (the pan-Canadian suicide prevention service), which was supported by funding announced in Budget 2019.
People reaching out to 9-8-8 were connected to a responder in a timely way and gained resources, knowledge and skills to help them cope with suicide crises and/or emotional distress.
Performance indicators:
- 86% of serviceable calls and 97% of serviceable texts were answered Footnote 1
- The average wait time before a call was answered by a responder was 56 seconds, and the average wait time before a text was answered by a responder was one minute and 53 seconds Footnote 2
- The average length of connected calls was 15 minutes and 57 seconds, and the average length of connected texts was 35 minutes and 07 seconds Footnote 3 .

### Aboriginal Head Start in Urban and Northern Communities
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2024–25
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Aboriginal Head Start in Urban and Northern Communities (AHSUNC) program supports Indigenous organizations in providing culturally appropriate early childhood development programs for First Nations, Inuit and Métis children and their families who live off reserve in urban and northern communities.
- 3,586 Footnote 4 Indigenous children and their families participated in activities at AHSUNC sites;
- 85% of AHSUNC sites leveraged multi-sectoral collaborations. Organizations from various sectors collaborate with AHSUNC sites to support the needs of AHSUNC participants; and
- 100% of children enrolled in AHSUNC experienced developmental benefits in a context that celebrates Indigenous cultures and language.

Planned: No planned audits

Planned: The next evaluation is planned for 2027–28
Engagement of applicants and recipients in 2024–25:
Recipients are engaged through targeted solicitations for ongoing program funding. Funded recipients are expected to deliver high-quality, culturally appropriate, community-based early learning programming for Indigenous children and families focused on six program components: Indigenous culture and language, education and school readiness, health promotion, nutrition, social support and parental involvement. PHAC officials continue to provide support and guidance to recipients through the implementation, monitoring and evaluation of their projects with the shared goal of advancing the broader objectives of the Indigenous Early Learning and Child Care (IELCC) Framework .
In implementing this framework through horizontal collaboration, PHAC's AHSUNC-IELCC Partnership Strategy guides engagement with applicants and recipient partners. This active and ongoing outreach supports partners' involvement in existing IELCC processes to contribute to holistic, Indigenous-led decision outcomes, including the development of allocation methodologies, results-based frameworks, and future priority setting.

### Canada Prenatal Nutrition Program
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2017–18
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Canada Prenatal Nutrition Program (CPNP) is intended to improve the health of pregnant women and people and their infants who face challenges that may put their health at risk. Funded community-level projects are aimed at increasing healthy birth weights, promoting and supporting healthy nutrition during pregnancy and postpartum, promoting and supporting breastfeeding, and supporting positive health behaviours and well-being. This program also promotes the creation of partnerships within communities and strengthens community capacity to increase support for pregnant women and people and new parents and guardians who face challenges that may put their health at risk.
In 2022–23, the CPNP provided programming to approximately 41,000 participants, including pregnant and postpartum women and people, and other parents and guardians. The CPNP was shown to leverage partnerships and additional funding sources, as demonstrated through the following results:
- 63% of projects worked with more than three different types of partners in 2022–23. CPNP projects partnered most frequently with health organizations, food/clothing banks or similar organizations and community organizations; and
- 41% of projects were able to leverage funds from other sources such as provincial, territorial, regional, or municipal governments in 2022–23.
In 2025, participants in the CPNP program were invited to respond to an online survey. Results showed that the program is contributing to building knowledge and skills of program participants, which support pre- and post-natal, child, and family health. For example, as a result of participating in CPNP:
- 89% of survey respondents reported knowing more about the importance of breastfeeding;
- 87% of survey respondents reported they have a better understanding of the effects of drinking alcohol during pregnancy on their baby; and
- 87% of survey respondents reported eating healthier foods.
Additional evidence showed that 94% of survey respondents reported that their health and well-being improved and 92% reported having more people to talk to when they need support as a result of participating in the CPNP program.

### Community Action Program for Children
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2017–18
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Community Action Program for Children (CAPC) aims to promote the health and development of children aged 0 to 6 years who face challenges that may put their health at risk. Funded community-level projects aim to develop and deliver a range of comprehensive and culturally appropriate early intervention and prevention activities aimed at improving health behaviours (such as positive parenting, parent-child attachment, healthy eating, and physical activity) and overall self-reported general health and well-being. This program also promotes the creation of partnerships within communities and strengthens community capacity to increase support for children and their families who face challenges that may put their health at risk.
In 2022–23 Footnote 5 , CAPC provided services to over 191,000 participants.
The CAPC has been successful in leveraging partnerships and additional funding sources. For example:
- 71% of CAPC projects worked with more than three different types of partners in 2022–23. CAPC projects partnered most frequently with health organizations, community organizations, and library/literacy organizations; and
- 56% of projects were able to leverage funds from other sources such as provincial, territorial, regional, or municipal governments in 2022–23.
In 2025, participants in the CAPC program were invited to respond to an online survey. Results showed that the program is contributing to building knowledge and skills of parents and caregivers, which support child and family health. For example, as a result of participating in CAPC:
- 88% of survey respondents reported their parenting skills had improved;
- 86% of survey respondents reported knowing more about how to keep their child healthy; and
- 84% of survey respondents reported that their child is better able to express themselves.
Additional evidence showed that 84% of survey respondents reported their child's health and well-being improved, 88% reported having a better relationship with their child and 92% reported having more people to talk to when they need support as a result of participating in the CAPC program.

### Contribution Funding for the Centre for Aging and Brain Health Innovation
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2022–23
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
This program helps innovators to accelerate the development, dissemination, scalability and promotion of promising aging and brain health innovations. The Centre for Aging and Brain Health Innovation (CABHI) supports innovators through an integrated suite of programs to help accelerate the innovation-to-commercialization process. CABHI does this by providing financial support for innovation projects, facilitating co-development with end-users (e.g., patients, caregivers and healthcare providers) and offering tailored services for innovators (e.g., scientific advice, training in innovation and health systems and developing procurement options and marketing plans).
In 2024–25, outcomes of the CABHI program included:
- Increased and accelerated development, validation and scaling of solutions that enable older adults and people living with brain health issues, including dementia, to live in the setting of their choice and remain active in their communities;
- Improved products, programs and services that support dementia prevention and enhanced the quality of life of people living with dementia and caregivers;
- Expanded delivery of high-quality, accessible virtual care for physical, mental, and cognitive health services and supports; and
- Enhanced knowledge mobilization related to aging and brain health.
Performance indicators:
- 58 projects approved and launched
- 61 unique solutions funded within CABHI's portfolio of innovation
- 90,605 individuals engaged in testing/validation with CABHI projects (e.g., community members participating in research studies as part of an intervention trial, technology deployment trial)
- 180 unique members to CABHI's Lived Experience Advisory Panel
- 134 solutions introduced into practice, brought to market, or adopted by care provider organizations
- 115 solutions successfully procured, acquired first-customers, or adopted subsequent to their engagement with a CABHI project or program
- 140 jobs created – not including CABHI personnel
- $425,527,211 of secured investments (public and private) by CABHI-funded innovators/companies.

### COVID-19 Proof of Vaccination Fund for Provinces and Territories
Type of transfer payment: Statutory
Type of appropriation: Not applicable
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: Not applicable
Link to departmental result:
- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
PHAC developed a Canadian COVID-19 Proof of Vaccination Credential (PVC) specification that is issued by provinces and territories. This initiative required extensive federal, provincial, territorial, and Indigenous engagement.
Building on this work, PHAC also established and administered the federal $300 million COVID-19 Proof of Vaccination Fund to support provinces and territories for costs incurred to implement the PVC programs. An exchange of ministerial letters is required to access the Fund.
Ontario accessed the fund in 2024–25, representing a $75.9 million transfer, and was the last jurisdiction to receive its allocation.
To date, all 13 provinces and territories have accessed the fund, representing $300 million in transfer payments. Administration of the fund is now complete.

### Healthy Canadians and Communities Fund
Type of transfer payment: Grant and Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2013–14
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.3: Chronic diseases are prevented
Link to the department's program inventory:
- Chronic Disease Prevention
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Healthy Canadians and Communities Fund (HCCF) focuses on promoting healthy living among Canadians who face health inequalities and are at greater risk of developing the main chronic diseases of diabetes, cardiovascular disease and cancer. The HCCF supports interventions that address behavioural risk factors for chronic diseases, including physical inactivity, unhealthy eating, and tobacco use. The program also aims to create physical and social environments that are known to support better health among Canadians.
HCCF priorities include investing in interventions that address health inequalities among priority populations, encouraging the participation of multiple sectors in chronic disease prevention and uncovering new ways to address risk factors for chronic disease.
In 2024–25, the HCCF provided funding to 46 projects. This included funding for new projects that started in that year, projects which began in previous years and continued to be implemented in 2024–25, and those that ended in 2024–25. Program results for the HCCF includes:
- 47% of project participants reported increased knowledge of chronic diseases or risk/protective factors;
- 53% of project participants demonstrated improved skills or abilities to support healthy behaviours;
- 69% of project participants reported that their social, physical, or food environments improved to support ongoing healthy behaviours and/or well-being;
- 53% of project participants reported improvements in their health behaviours; and
- 57% of project participants reported improvements in their health status.

### HIV and Hepatitis C Community Action Fund
Type of transfer payment: Grant and Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19
Link to departmental result:
- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
- Communicable Disease and Infection Control
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of the HIV and Hepatitis C Community Action Fund (CAF) program is to reduce the incidence and health impacts of sexually transmitted and blood-borne infections (STBBI) in Canada.
Aligned with the Pan-Canadian Framework for Action on STBBI and the Government of Canada Five Year Action Plan on STBBI , the HIV and Hepatitis C CAF seeks to ensure that:
- community-based efforts reach key populations, including individuals unaware of their HIV/hepatitis C status, and link them to testing, prevention, treatment and care;
- communities design and implement evidence-informed frontline projects to prevent new and reoccurring infections;
- high-impact interventions are brought to scale so that more people benefit from them; and
- community-based efforts reduce stigma toward populations disproportionately affected by STBBI, including people living with HIV or hepatitis C.
Community-based organizations play a critical role in delivering sexual health education and prevention activities, developing culturally adapted resources, and conducting community capacity-building activities due to their expertise in identifying and implementing culturally and contextually appropriate solutions. With the HIV and Hepatitis C CAF, PHAC funds projects and initiatives that focus on the following key populations:
- Gay, bisexual men and other men who have sex with men
- People who use drugs
- First Nations, Inuit and Métis communities
- Racialized people and migrants, particularly from regions with high HIV or hepatitis C prevalence
- Sex workers and their clients
- People living in or recently released from correctional facilities
- Transgender and non-binary people
- People living with HIV or hepatitis C
- Women among these groups, as appropriate
- Youth among these groups, as appropriate
In 2024–25, PHAC provided $26.4 million through the HIV and Hepatitis C CAF to support over 130 community-based projects across Canada. These time-limited projects aim to address HIV, hepatitis C, and other sexually transmitted and blood-borne infections. The final impact data for 2024–25 is being gathered and preliminary data indicate that over 48,000 individuals from key populations and approximately 13,400 individuals from target audiences were reached in 2023–24 across the country.
The Canadian AIDS Treatment Information Exchange (CATIE) is funded under the HIV and Hepatitis C CAF. As Canada's National STBBI Knowledge Broker, its role is to expedite the translation of STBBI-related knowledge into frontline policies, practices and programs that impact key populations across Canada.
- distributed over 560,000 resources to support frontline service providers, community organizations, public health professionals, healthcare providers, and health policy decision-makers;
- facilitated the delivery of 47 courses on HIV and hepatitis C to over 2,000 learners and hosted 12 webinars on syphilis, reaching about 3,200 attendees;
- successfully contributed to the outcomes of the CAF, reporting that: 98% of respondents from target audiences reported an increased knowledge of effective evidence-based HIV, hepatitis C and related STBBI prevention measures; 65% of respondents from target audiences reported using strategies to prevent HIV, hepatitis C or related STBBIs, including 47% reporting condom use, 40% getting vaccinated for HPV or HBV, and 39% reporting using new drug-use equipment; 87% of respondents from target audiences reported a strengthened capacity to provide culturally responsive and culturally safe STBBI prevention, testing, treatment and care services; and 71% of respondents from target audiences reported a change in policy or practice made by themselves or their organization to improve the cultural safety and stigma-free nature of STBBI testing, prevention, treatment, and ongoing care and support services.
- 98% of respondents from target audiences reported an increased knowledge of effective evidence-based HIV, hepatitis C and related STBBI prevention measures;
- 65% of respondents from target audiences reported using strategies to prevent HIV, hepatitis C or related STBBIs, including 47% reporting condom use, 40% getting vaccinated for HPV or HBV, and 39% reporting using new drug-use equipment;
- 87% of respondents from target audiences reported a strengthened capacity to provide culturally responsive and culturally safe STBBI prevention, testing, treatment and care services; and
- 71% of respondents from target audiences reported a change in policy or practice made by themselves or their organization to improve the cultural safety and stigma-free nature of STBBI testing, prevention, treatment, and ongoing care and support services.
In 2024–25, an additional $1.5 million in time limited, community-based funding supported organizations with expertise in sexually transmitted infections and harm reduction services to reach key populations affected by rising rates of infectious and congenital syphilis. Through this funding, community-led activities took place, including two national interventions aimed at reducing congenital syphilis among women and their partners, and infectious syphilis among gay, bisexual, and other men who have sex with men. The following resources were developed through these efforts:
- a syphilis client resource for Indigenous individuals;
- trans-inclusive STBBI testing resources; and
- an e-learning module and webinar focused on syphilis.
The Community-Based Research Centre (CBRC), developed a pan-Canadian strategy to raise awareness about syphilis testing among young gay men and individuals engaged in sexual activities while under the influence of psychoactive drugs, also known as chemsex. The initiative emphasized sexual health literacy, reinfection risks, and syphilis and HIV prevention strategies such as pre-and post-exposure prophylaxes. CBRC also produced two bilingual print and digital information resources and advertisements. A regional intervention led by the AIDS Committee of Newfoundland and Labrador focused on increasing awareness of both public and health care providers of congenital and infectious syphilis. The campaign used culturally safe, low-barrier messaging on symptoms, testing, contact tracing, and prevention of congenital syphilis. It was paired with educational information for healthcare providers and developed in consultation with 274 representatives from healthcare, government, and community sectors.
The Manitoba Harm Reduction Network implemented a peer-led syphilis testing initiative. This included:
- "Test and Treat" pop-up events in Winnipeg and northern Manitoba; and
- prenatal care educational events aimed at preventing congenital syphilis.
These community outreach activities were designed to improve early detection and treatment of syphilis among pregnant individuals and other high-risk populations.

### Immunization Partnership Fund
Type of transfer payment: Grant and Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2023–24
Link to departmental result:
- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Immunization Partnership Fund (IPF) is a key component of PHAC's efforts to support confidence and increase uptake of vaccines across the life course to control and prevent the spread of vaccine-preventable diseases. The IPF is an important lever to identify and help address gaps in vaccine acceptance. It is an equity-based program that aims to empower Canadians to make informed vaccine decisions, particularly those from communities or populations experiencing inequities which prevent them from accessing vaccination or vaccine education. The program invests in projects across Canada that support community-centered vaccination education, promotion and outreach activities among priority populations, and that build the capacity of healthcare service providers to better meet the needs of priority populations. These equity-promoting initiatives provide culturally safe, evidence-informed and low-barrier access to vaccination and vaccine education.
In 2024–25 the IPF provided $5 million to 38 community-based and national organizations to develop and deliver vaccine confidence and uptake activities in communities across the country. Projects receiving funding share a focus on populations who experience social and structural inequities. This work includes projects that support catching up on routine vaccines that were missed or delayed due to the pandemic. Funded projects also empower communities to make informed vaccination decisions for themselves and their families by de-bunking and pre-bunking mis- and disinformation, and by providing non-judgemental and culturally safe environments for dialogue.
In 2024–25, funding recipients developed or adapted more than 260 unique vaccination resources, activities and services for people across Canada, based on an evidence-informed, equity-promoting and culturally safe approach. By implementing barrier-reducing approaches, IPF projects engaged with more than 69,000 individuals through in-person and virtual programming efforts and hosted more than 630 vaccination clinics, resulting in more than 7,800 individuals receiving a vaccine.

Findings of evaluations completed in 2024–25:
An evaluation of the Vaccination program was completed in 2024-2025 but approved in early April 2025.
Summary of Findings:
The evaluation found that PHAC has made significant progress towards achieving its expected outcomes. PHAC provided evidence-based information to partners, stakeholders and Canadians to make informed choices about vaccination. As well, while delivering vaccinations to Canadians is a provincial/territorial responsibility, PHAC supported timely and equitable access to vaccines and vaccination information through various initiatives such as the Bulk Procurement Program and the Immunization Partnership Fund. Despite PHAC's dedicated efforts, key vaccination coverage rates are below target and PHAC, like other public health authorities around the world, is facing the significant challenges of rising vaccine skepticism and widespread vaccine mis- and disinformation. The evaluation included a recommendation to explore opportunities to share the Immunization Partnership Fund best practices and lessons learned.

This allowed recipients to share experiences in promoting routine and seasonal vaccination across the life course, and share strategies for raising vaccine awareness, improving access, and addressing successes and challenges experienced in project implementation. Additionally, recipients were engaged through ongoing performance measurement and quarterly meetings. The IPF also created the IPF newsletter, which has been expanded in scope and audience to provide updates on IPF projects and share content relevant to IPF recipients and their networks to support evidence-based project implementation. The newsletter is published on a quarterly cycle.

### Immunization Surveillance
Type of transfer payment: Grant and Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2022–23
Link to departmental result:
- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of the Immunization Surveillance program is to bolster Canadian scientific capacity and preparedness to enhance the evidence base on vaccine coverage, safety, and effectiveness. This informs the implementation of programs and targeted interventions while complementing existing surveillance systems by filling evidence gaps identified through regular surveillance activities.
The 2024–25 investment of $7 million provided continued support to five projects investigating long-term outcomes of post-vaccination adverse events. The funding:
- advanced the understanding of new or merging adverse events following immunization in a Canadian context;
- enhanced capacity within Canada to monitor vaccine coverage within specific underserved populations; and
- gave PHAC a greater ability to mobilize external resources quickly and effectively to address emerging vaccine safety issues or other immunization surveillance evidence gaps.
The five projects funded by the Immunization Surveillance program in 2024–25 were:
The McMaster University project (funded since 2021), which:
- leveraged the world-renowned Platelet Immunology Laboratory to contribute to the vaccine-induced immune thrombotic thrombocytopenia (VITT) knowledge base, publishing over five articles and delivering one presentation; and
- raised awareness of VITT through publications and presentations, offered guidance for its diagnosis and management and advanced the understanding of VITT disease mechanisms, and longer-term outcomes.
The Canadian Cardiovascular Society (funded since 2022), which:
- continued its MYCOVACC surveillance study of myocarditis and pericarditis following mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in 2024–25, expanding the number of study sites to over 30 across Canada, allowing for a nationally representative patient sample for both pediatric and adult patients; and
- through the Libin Cardiovascular Institute in Calgary, led a multi-centre cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) study, including sites in BC, AB, ON, and QC, with results presented at the Canadian Cardiovascular Congress 2024 workshop. This project continues to play an important role in educating the public and health practitioners about myo-pericarditis, which is especially important for vaccine confidence related to mRNA vaccines.
The Canadian National Vaccine Safety Network (CANVAS) at Dalhousie University (funded since 2023), which:
- conducted active vaccine safety surveillance for COVID-19, influenza and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) vaccines as part of a vaccine safety project by estimating the frequency of severe Adverse Events Following Immunization (AEFI); and
- was a world leader in enhanced, participant-based vaccine safety monitoring, collecting survey data from over 1 million participants who had received COVID vaccines, influenza vaccines, RSV vaccines and RSV monoclonal antibodies.
The Acute Respiratory Illness (ARI) Sentinel Practitioner Surveillance Network (SPSN) at the Provincial Health Services Authority of British Columbia (funded since 2023), which:
- monitored COVID-19, influenza and non-influenza respiratory viruses to provide vaccine effectiveness estimates to public health and immunization stakeholders and the Canadian public;
- enrolled about 400 sentinel sites in four provinces (AB, BC, ON, QC) and trained about 1,000 practitioners;
- detected 20 respiratory viruses in the reporting year through their periodic surveillance reporting, including influenza, RSV and SARS-CoV-2;
- enrolled and processed about 10,000 specimens, among which, 3,000 influenza viruses and 500 SARS-CoV-2 viruses were identified, with successful sequencing and genetic characterization of over 60% of respective case viruses; and
- generated data used by Canada's Chief Public Health Officer and international bodies, including the World Health Organization and countries in the southern hemisphere, to inform decisions on formulation of vaccines for upcoming influenza seasons.
The University of Ottawa's Interdisciplinary Centre for Black Health (funded since 2023), which:
- examined vaccine effectiveness and immunization coverage in racialized equity-denied communities through a project entitled Perceptions of Vaccine Effectiveness and Immunization Coverage: A Comparative Study of Risk and Protective Factors in Racialized and Ethnic Populations in Canada; and
- created and disseminated information sheets, reaching over 327,000 people across Canada, and organized 16 public meetings and two conferences. By making complex information accessible in plain language, this project contributed to building trust in health systems and combatting misinformation.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Indigenous Early Learning and Child Care (IELCC) Transformation Initiative supports the implementation of the co-developed Indigenous Early Learning and Child Care Framework. This framework reflects the unique cultures and priorities of First Nations, Inuit, and Métis children across Canada.
This initiative enables greater control in IELCC through a new partnership model to facilitate Indigenous-led decision-making to advance national and regional priorities.
Employment and Social Development Canada (ESDC) is the federal lead for this horizontal initiative. Indigenous Services Canada and PHAC are key partners.
The IELCC Transformation Initiative contributed to the shared objective of providing high quality early learning and childcare services that are also affordable, flexible, and inclusive, as outlined in ESDC's Horizontal Management Framework.
The IELCC Transformation Initiative contributed to achieving results through reporting on the number of quality improvement projects funded that:
- enabled the development of curriculum content incorporating Indigenous traditions, cultures, and languages;
- built community, administration, professional capacity, and centres of expertise; and
- supported staff training and other activities that will enhance access to high quality IELCC.
The IELCC Transformation Initiative will also demonstrate progress on the shared outcome through reporting on the number of children accessing culturally appropriate and inclusive IELCC, with the target to be determined with PHAC (baseline 4,500 children).

The Horizontal Evaluation of IELCC was completed in 2024–25
Summary of Findings:
The horizontal evaluation of IELCC Initiative, led by ESDC, found that programs such as Aboriginal Head Start in Urban and Northern Communities (AHSUNC) and Aboriginal Head Start on Reserve are contributing meaningfully to the quality, cultural relevance, and accessibility of IELCC, with strong evidence of support for cultural identity, school readiness, and holistic child development. The evaluation also highlighted persistent challenges, including limited infrastructure, stagnant funding, and disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. The observations put forward stated that continued collaboration with Indigenous partners is essential to improve performance measurement and data systems, and that simplifying federal processes could enhance coordination and support the long-term goal of transferring control of Early Learning and Child Care to Indigenous Peoples.

This active and ongoing outreach supports partners' involvement in existing IELCC processes to contribute to holistic, Indigenous-led decision outcomes, including the development of allocation methodologies, results-based frameworks, and future priority setting.

### Mental Health Promotion Innovation Fund
Type of transfer payment: Grant and Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Mental Health Promotion Innovation Fund (MHPIF) is a funding program that replaced the Innovation Strategy in 2019–20. The current stream of funding aims to promote positive mental health for children, youth, their caregivers and communities, as well as reduce systemic barriers for population mental health in Canada. The program uses a multi-phased approach to fund the testing and delivery of innovative, community-based interventions that aim to advance health equity, build protective factors and address the underlying determinants of health at the population level. Knowledge gained from the evaluation of each community-based intervention is then applied to public health policy and practice to accelerate change for positive mental health in Canada. The MHPIF also funds a Knowledge Development and Exchange (KDE) Hub that supports funded projects in sharing lessons learned, connecting with various stakeholders and applying new knowledge to a broader policy and system change agenda.
The Mental Health Promotion Innovation Fund funded the testing of 20 population health interventions in Phase 1, reaching over 50,000 individuals across the country. In 2022–23, 15 of these projects moved to Phase 2 to fully implement and evaluate their intervention. Since 2019, the MHP-IF projects have reached 324,901 children, youth, families, and practitioners in over 800 communities across Canada. In 2023–24 alone, these projects reached over 160,000 children, youth, caregivers, and families from over 800 communities across Canada. Early results from Phase 2 demonstrate that projects are impacting the protective factors to promote mental health including increased social-emotional development in children, increased coping, improved school engagement, and improved mental health literacy. In addition, projects have moved beyond individual change to impact broader mental health promotion policies and practices, resulting in sustainable change for the communities in which they work.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The National Collaborating Centres (NCCs) for Public Health program increases public health system capacity in Canada by improving the ability of research to be applied and implemented in public health settings.
The objective of this program is to promote evidence-informed decision-making by public health practitioners and policy makers across Canada. The NCCs synthesize and share knowledge in ways that are useful and accessible to public health stakeholders.
In 2024–25, the NCCs for Public Health celebrated their 20th anniversary, marking an important milestone in knowledge translation to tackle emerging and evolving public health issues and knowledge gaps. The Centres were created in 2004 in response to the SARS outbreak to strengthen the public health system. Their ongoing contribution and responsiveness to the evolving public health needs is a testament to value of their work. Key achievements in 2024–25 include:
- The six NCCs collectively finalized the Update of the Core Competencies for Public Health in Canada. The project's success hinged on broad and diverse engagement, mobilized by the NCCs through their collaborations and extensive networks at local, regional, provincial/territorial, and federal levels. The updated set of competencies will strengthen the public health workforce, with a renewed commitment to equity, excellence, and collective action in public health practice and education.
- The NCCs increased public health capacity through the development of a variety of knowledge mobilization tools for targeted public health audiences including: disseminating over 1,275 products (published materials, videos, workshops, webinars, online courses, blogs, guidance documents, case studies, environmental scans, rapid reviews, and conference presentations) conducting 648 knowledge translation activities which supported practitioners and decision makers in applying new knowledge in their environments undertaking over 328 knowledge-related needs and gaps identification activities to provide public health knowledge brokers with the resources and structures required to strengthen evidence-informed decision-making.
- disseminating over 1,275 products (published materials, videos, workshops, webinars, online courses, blogs, guidance documents, case studies, environmental scans, rapid reviews, and conference presentations)
- conducting 648 knowledge translation activities which supported practitioners and decision makers in applying new knowledge in their environments
- undertaking over 328 knowledge-related needs and gaps identification activities to provide public health knowledge brokers with the resources and structures required to strengthen evidence-informed decision-making.
- The NCCs engaged and maintained over 693 partnerships and collaborations, facilitating and increasing public health outreach and exchange. Among them, 87 collaborations targeted emerging public health issues.
Knowledge mobilization examples include:
- The NCC for Environmental Health conducted evidence reviews on critical topics such as wildfire interventions: air cleaners and masks as well as completing the first-ever pan-Canadian inventory of heat alert and response systems.
- The NCC for Infectious Diseases expanded reach and success of peer-to-peer learning on wastewater-based surveillance for public health.
- The NCC for Indigenous Health led the design and delivery of an Indigenous virtual gathering on science and pandemic preparedness and response.
- The NCC for Methods and Tools responded to 16 public health questions via evidence summaries on important emergent issues such as using AI in evidence reviews, and the effects of wearing masks, or air filtration systems and known effective public health messaging related to wildfires. This year the center prioritized tailored support for rural, remote, and indigenous communities through focused resources, and customized initiatives (e.g., incorporation of non-Western and Indigenous Ways of Knowing into evidence-informed decision making (EIDM)). In addition, the center engaged with pan-Canadian students in public health related post-secondary public health programs to build their capacity for EIDM, supporting their future careers.
- The NCC for Healthy Public Policy continued the implementation and development of the Canadian Network for Health in All Policies.
- The NCC for Determinants of Health fostered shared learning and knowledge mobilization through convening of formal networks such as the Health Equity Collaborative Network for shared learning and mobilization.

### National Microbiology Laboratory – Wastewater Innovative Technologies
Type of transfer payment: Contribution

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
Wastewater testing can provide an early warning signal to inform public health action (e.g., vaccination programs, masking). The purpose of this program is to implement and expand wastewater monitoring for SARS-CoV-2 and other priority pathogens (e.g. antimicrobial resistance, RSV, and flu) across Canada. This contribution provides funding to partners from provincial, territorial, and municipal governments and academia across Canada to develop laboratory methods in the wastewater surveillance network to build capacity, conduct laboratory testing, wastewater sampling, data modelling, and knowledge translation.
The National Wastewater Monitoring Program contribution agreements supported the implementation of wastewater surveillance across provinces and territories, including surveillance projects at borders and airports. In 2024–25, the program continued to support testing capacity for COVID-19, influenza, and RSV across 68 community sites throughout Canada and at airports. These efforts sustained national surveillance coverage and enhanced early detection capabilities.
Progress also continued under the 27 contribution agreements established between 2022 and 2025, which addressed several priority areas. Activities focused on increasing wastewater testing and enhancing or expanding wastewater monitoring capacity in provinces, territories, and Indigenous communities; increasing surveillance at border and airports; as well as building on the research and development foundation required for the timely detection and monitoring of COVID-19. Efforts were also sustained to expand monitoring to other priority pathogens (e.g., antimicrobial resistance targets, RSV) through academic research projects, and to strengthen knowledge across jurisdictions. Academic research projects funded through the program made progress in developing and enhancing assays for respiratory diseases, such as COVID-19 and other pathogens.
In 2024–25, the wastewater testing hub in Atlantic Canada continued to support regional testing needs, while ongoing partnership with First Nations communities helped expand local wastewater monitoring activities. The wastewater-based surveillance peer-learning program, led by the Canadian Water Network (CWN), in collaboration with the National Collaborating Centre for Infectious Diseases (NCCID), continued its work to equip public health practitioners with skills to effectively use wastewater data to inform decision making by hosting a second national in-person summit in September 2024. From this, knowledge products were produced drawing on success stories heard. PHAC also maintained collaboration with provincial and territorial partners to host their wastewater data on PHAC's Wastewater monitoring dashboard , supporting data sharing and transparency.

### Pan-Canadian Vaccine Injury Support Program
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Pan-Canadian Vaccine Injury Support Program (VISP) ensures that all Canadians who experience a serious and permanent injury as a result of receiving a Health Canada-authorized vaccine, administered in Canada on or after December 8, 2020, have fair and timely access to financial support. The exception are people vaccinated in Québec who will receive coverage from the longstanding Québec program.
An independent administrator was selected via an open call for applications to administer the VISP (except for Québec) using contribution funding. Québec is continuing to deliver its longstanding Vaccine Injury Compensation program with federal contribution funding.
The program, intended for individuals vaccinated in all provinces except Québec, was administered at arm's length from PHAC through a contribution agreement, totalling $16.1 million for 2024–25. Funding was also provided to the province of Québec through a separate contribution agreement, totaling $2.2 million during the same period.
2024–25 marked the fourth year of operations for the VISP, and PHAC continued to monitor the delivery of the program. Reviews of claims under the program are carried out by a team of medical experts, using internationally accepted industry processes. Establishing the causality between a vaccine and an injury and determining the severity and permanency of an injury is complex and can sometimes be a lengthy process.
As a mechanism to inform Canadians about the progress of claims processing and program activity in 2024–25, updated program statistics were posted twice to the VISP website and once to the VIC website.
In 2024–25, PHAC continued its outreach to increase awareness of the program among health care professionals, who are best placed to support those who experience a serious and permanent injury to access the program.
In anticipation of a planned review of the program in year five (2025–26), PHAC engaged academic experts in 2024–25 to conduct an independent study of comparable international injury compensation programs and review their relevant lessons learned and best practices to help inform the future program design and ensure fair, efficient, and cost-effective delivery that meets the needs of Canadians.

### Preventing Family Violence
Type of transfer payment: Grant and Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19
Link to departmental results:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
- Result 1.2: Canadians have improved health behaviours
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Preventing Family Violence Program Footnote 9 invests in the delivery and testing of health promotion programs and interventions that prevent family violence and improve health outcomes for survivors of family violence including child maltreatment, youth dating violence, intimate partner violence, and the mistreatment of older adults. Some interventions work with participants directly while others equip health and social service professionals to respond safely and effectively to family violence.
The ultimate intended outcome of the program is to increase the uptake of health promotion interventions that are effective in preventing family violence and addressing its health impacts. This is achieved by funding intervention research that builds the evidence base on what works, for whom and where, using trauma- and violence-informed approaches. This program contributes to the federal Gender-based Violence Strategy .
In 2024–25, PHAC funded 70 projects under the Preventing Family Violence Program, including six grants. The funded projects included 26 new funding agreements, signed as a result of calls for proposals on youth dating violence, child maltreatment prevention, public health approaches to child welfare, and building service provider capacity to safely respond to family violence. For example:
- Family Service Saskatoon is delivering and testing a health promotion intervention for Saskatoon youth to promote healthy relationships and prevent youth dating violence with a focus on youth that have experienced trauma in their early years, as well as newcomer, racialized, and 2SLGBTQI+ youth, and youth that have experienced gender-based violence.
- The Martin Family Initiative is adapting, delivering, and evaluating their health and family well-being resources to better support early childhood professionals to promote positive parenting and ultimately contribute to the reduction of child maltreatment with the Indigenous populations they serve across Canada.
- The University of Ottawa is working with shelters across Quebec to increase the knowledge of child protection policies and procedures in order to better assist service providers in offering support to individuals and families who have experienced intimate partner violence.
PHAC continued to support 26 existing projects that are delivering and testing health promotion interventions aimed at preventing and addressing family violence, such as:
- The Muriel McQueen Fergusson Centre for Family Violence Research at the University of New Brunswick is adapting and evaluating an advocacy intervention for women in midlife and older who experience intimate partner violence in three Maritime provinces: Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, and Prince Edward Island. The intervention is tailored to cover safety planning, information on violence cycles, community and legal resources, and support for women to develop future goals and strategies.
- MediaSmarts is developing, delivering, and testing a digital literacy program for survivors of family violence in transitional housing by providing online safety information and educational content to prevent technology-facilitated violence.
PHAC funds two communities of practice that amplify the overall impact of funded projects through networking, knowledge mobilization, and capacity building:
- The Knowledge Hub , hosted by Western University's Centre for Education on Violence Against Women and Children , connects and enhances intervention research projects focused on family violence prevention.
- The Promoting Relationships & Eliminating Violence Network (PREVNet) at the University of Calgary enhances collaboration and consolidates learning from projects focused on youth dating violence prevention.
This was also the last year of funding for 10 projects focused on youth dating violence, child maltreatment, and intimate partner violence. For example:
- The McCreary Centre Society from the University of British Columbia developed, implemented and evaluated Taking Pride , a healthy relationship program for sexual and gender minority youth throughout British Columbia.
- The Child Development Institute hosted and facilitated a community of practice to support organizations that are delivering the Stop Now And Plan behavioural program across the country.
- The Women's Centre for Social Justice adapted, piloted, and evaluated a multi-sectoral intervention for persons who self-identify as women survivors of intimate partner violence with resultant brain injury.
Available results from funded projects that are complete or are nearing the end of their funding period show that participants report increased knowledge and skills relating to healthy relationships and positive parenting, as well as positive changes in behaviour when they put these skills to use. Projects working to build the capacity of service providers and professionals report very high levels of satisfaction with the resources and training, with nearly all professionals acquiring knowledge and skills and a majority applying them in their professional practice. The evidence from effective interventions continued to inform policies and programs.
PHAC also conducted an extensive review of the academic literature on family violence in order to better understand the contributions of PHAC-funded projects on the evidence base for family violence prevention in Canada. This analysis showed that PHAC-funded projects generated 17% of all peer-reviewed articles on family violence prevention published in Canada from 2014 to 2024, accounting for a significant contribution to Canadian research and evidence.
Finally, a new solicitation was launched in March 2025 to support up to twenty-five projects to prevent and address child maltreatment through the delivery and testing of health promotion interventions that build skills and knowledge for safe and healthy relationships, equip service providers with evidence-based training and resources, and/or address factors that put families and children at risk of child maltreatment. Up to twenty-five projects are anticipated to be supported.

### Strengthening the Canadian Drugs and Substances Strategy (Harm Reduction Fund)
Type of transfer payment: Grant and Contribution Footnote 10
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19
Link to departmental results:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
- Result 1.2: Canadians have improved health behaviours
- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
- Communicable Diseases and Infection Control
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Canadian Drugs and Substances Strategy (CDSS) is the Government of Canada's comprehensive, collaborative, compassionate and evidence-informed approach to drug policy, which uses a public health lens when considering and addressing substance use. The CDSS covers a broad range of legal and illegal substances, including cannabis, alcohol, opioids and other substances. The goal of the strategy is to protect the health and safety of all Canadians by minimizing harms from substance use for individuals, families and communities.
The CDSS recognizes that a national approach to substance use requires strong collaboration and coordination among all levels of government, working in their respective areas of jurisdiction. It also recognizes the critical importance of working closely with a wide range of stakeholders on an ongoing basis, including people with lived and living experience with substance use. Reducing the stigma experienced by people who use drugs is also integral to the CDSS and an ongoing priority for the Government of Canada.
As a partner department, PHAC receives contributions under the CDSS to support federal harm reduction efforts through the Harm Reduction Fund (HRF). Through the HRF, PHAC complements provincial and territorial harm reduction strategies by investing $7 million annually to support time-limited projects across Canada to help reduce HIV and hepatitis C among people who share injection and inhalation drug-use equipment. Harm reduction refers to a set of practices that aim to reduce the harms associated with substance use. Harm reduction:
- is about meeting people where they are at and supporting them to work towards their goals;
- aims to decrease adverse health, social and economic outcomes, such as disease and injury that may result from an individual's actions;
- represents policies, strategies, services and practices, which aim to assist people to live safer and healthier lives;
- acknowledges that each person is different, has different goals and requires different supports and strategies; and
- is not focused on the reduction of substance use or abstinence as a precursor to receive respect, compassion or services.
In 2024–25, PHAC provided $7 million through the HRF to fund 40 community-based projects aimed at addressing HIV, hepatitis C, and other sexually transmitted and blood-borne infections. While final impact data for 2024–25 are still being reported by projects, preliminary figures indicate that over 38,200 individuals who use and share inhalation or injection drug-use equipment and approximately 5,000 individuals from target audiences were reached in 2023–24.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Blood Safety Contribution Program (BSCP) supports the development and/or enhancement of provincial and territorial systems that monitor adverse events associated with the transfusion of blood and blood products and the transplantation of Cells, Tissues and Organs (CTOs). BSCP funding recipients include provincial and territorial governments, transfusion and transplantation centres, agencies or groups designated by provincial and territorial Ministries of Health to undertake surveillance for blood, tissue, organ-associated adverse events and Canadian not-for profit organizations that support transfusion and transplantation adverse event surveillance activities. Recipients of the funding then transfer information to PHAC via BSCP's Transfusion Error Surveillance System (TESS), Transfusion-Transmitted Injuries Surveillance System (TTISS) and Cells, Tissues and Organs Surveillance System (CTOSS). This information is prepared, analyzed and reported at a national level and made available to recipients and other stakeholders of the transfusion and transplantation system. This information is used to identify adverse event and error trends, to benchmark provincial/territorial adverse events against national-level data, and to make international comparisons. The surveillance information has also contributed to the development of transfusion guidelines to improve transfusion practices.
BSCP projects establish systems to monitor adverse events associated with transfusion and transplantation that could involve infectious diseases. As the use of blood, blood products and CTOs continues to increase in Canada, there is an elevated risk of adverse events. Monitoring adverse events will allow for more timely response in the event of a new or previously unknown blood and/or CTO safety issue.
In partnership with Canadian sentinel hospitals, PHAC is responsible for the collection, management and analysis of the TTISS, TESS and CTOSS data as well as the production of reports summarizing key findings. These data and products are used to inform public health policy action and support continuous improvement of PHAC's surveillance standards.
As the program is ending on March 31, 2026, publication of the final TTISS, TESS, and CTOSS reports and data archiving are underway.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
CARB-X (the Combating Antibiotic-Resistant Bacteria Biopharmaceutical Accelerator) is a leading global organization whose mission is to drive a diverse portfolio of innovative antibacterial products towards clinical development. CARB-X fills a critical gap to help stem the rise of AMR by focusing both on the most serious drug-resistant bacteria identified by the WHO and the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, as well as on the syndromes with the highest degrees of mortality and morbidity.
CARB-X is currently funded by a global consortium of governments and foundations, including the United States, the United Kingdom, Germany, the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation , and Wellcome Trust . It is also recognized by world leaders, including by G7 Health Ministers in their 2023 Communiqué and G20 Health Ministers in their 2023 Outcome Document .
Funding from Canada will support CARB-X's efforts in replenishing the global clinical pipeline with high-impact products which the world, including Canada, needs to address AMR in the years to come.
CARB-X completed an assessment of the Canadian innovation ecosystem for antibacterial product development and provided recommendations for Canada. CARB-X also strengthened relationships with researchers in Canada and increased awareness among Canadian product developers of the program and entry requirements for the CARB-X portfolio. Additionally, PHAC contributed to global pooled efforts to stimulate antimicrobial research and development.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Dementia Community Investment (DCI) supports community-based projects intended to optimize the health and well-being of people living with dementia, and family/friend caregivers, and increase knowledge of dementia and risk and protective factors. The program also provides funding support for a knowledge hub to facilitate collaboration among DCI projects and to help share lessons learned with the broader community.
In 2024–25, PHAC continued funding 10 community-based projects through the DCI. These projects were delivered in communities across Canada, including Alberta, British Columbia, Manitoba, Nova Scotia, Saskatchewan, as well as one national initiative. These projects implemented a variety of approaches to support people living with dementia and their family/friend caregivers. Examples include:
- a healthy lifestyle initiative aimed at promoting the health and well-being of people living with dementia and their caregivers;
- music-making interventions for older adults living with dementia and their family/friend caregivers;
- the distribution of tablets equipped with voice commands, reminders, and prompts to support daily living for people living with dementia; and
- a wellness and communication campaign sharing the lived experiences of caregivers and providing information on dementia risk and protective factors.
To date, 21 DCI projects have been completed. Of the 1,829 participants involved in these projects, 89% reported increased knowledge or skills, 82% reported making positive behavioural changes, and 69% reported improvements in protective factors.

Type of transfer payment: Grant

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program is to fund research that will fill knowledge gaps in scientific evidence to inform public health measures guidance and advice to Canadians regarding respiratory infectious diseases (such as COVID-19, the flu and RSV). This will help ensure that Canadian recommendations are based on current evidence thereby contributing to a stronger, more informed and more resilient public health system. Priority topics for each solicitation are identified through consultations and by aligning with national and international research priorities. Previous objectives included funding projects focused on investigating the effectiveness of public health measures for COVID-19 and those that examine the transmission dynamics of respiratory infectious diseases, including COVID-19, the flu and RSV.
Following a call for letters of intent in June 2024, PHAC awarded a total of $785,020 to fund four projects:
- The Hospital for Sick Children: Viral dynamics of influenza, RSV and SARS-CoV-2 in children and secondary transmission within schools and households
- The Governors of the University of Alberta: Particle characterization, breathing patterns, and biological sources of bioaerosols emitted from wearers of different mask/respirator types
- The University of Saskatchewan: Genomic factors contributing to virulence and transmissibility of highly pathogenic avian influenza viruses
- The University of British Columbia: The impact of contact rates and health behaviours on COVID-19 infection
Outcomes from this research are expected to inform federal policy regarding public health measures for respiratory infectious diseases and contribute Canadian data to the body of evidence on public health measures available to policy makers globally.
The final reports from these four projects are expected to be completed in March 2026.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder (FASD) National Strategic Projects Fund aims to reduce the number of individuals affected by prenatal alcohol exposure by supporting partnerships with community-based organizations, research institutions and provincial and territorial governments to develop and disseminate knowledge, tools and resources related to FASD prevention and awareness for use by health professionals and other frontline workers across the country.
The FASD National Strategic Projects Fund supported five projects that support FASD awareness, prevention, and stigma reduction. The projects continued to reach an audience of women and people of childbearing age, pregnant women and people and their partners, young adults, people with FASD, service providers, and policy makers.
In 2024–25, the project activities included:
- evaluating the effectiveness of a school-based FASD education and prevention curriculum that was modified, culturally adapted and then taught in select Ontario schools in First Nations and urban and rural communities;
- delivering a bilingual awareness campaign to prevent alcohol consumption during pregnancy and address stigma associated with FASD;
- developing FASD prevention messaging and resources for the public and health and allied professionals;
- delivering FASD training sessions for justice and legal professionals to 120 legal aid staff, as well as sessions for the general public, community service providers and clinicians;
- developing and launching an online self-paced FASD training for justice and legal professionals;
- developing a live and self-paced version of a FASD training curriculum for students in social work, medicine, and nursing programs in Atlantic Canada;
- developing resources and infographics with health promotion messaging around FASD awareness, prevention and supports intended for the general public and targeted groups;
- delivering a social media and radio ad campaign focused on FASD prevention and awareness, which is now available for download in four languages;
- hosting consultations across the country to support a comprehensive scientific assessment on FASD with the aim to strengthen PHAC's approach to address FASD and inform future priorities for action; and
- conducting a needs assessment and developing an online FASD training course tailored to the needs of frontline children's program workers.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Healthy Early Years (HEY) program supports communities to develop comprehensive, culturally and linguistically appropriate programs to improve the health and development of children (0 to 6 years). The program also intends to improve access to early childhood health promotion programming for children and their families living in Official Language Minority Communities (OLMCs) and is an initiative initially introduced under the 2018–2023 Action Plan for Official Languages , and continues to be part of the 2023–2028 Action Plan for Official Languages: Protection-Promotion-Collaboration .
The two HEY funding recipients funded 51 third party projects aimed at improving access to early childhood health promotion programming for families in situations of vulnerability living in OLMCs, with the goal of helping these populations acquire knowledge and skills, adopt positive healthy behaviours, and improve long-term health outcomes.
A variety of public health topics were addressed and promoted through funded projects with a focus on mental health of parents, guardians, and families and healthy child development. 20 of the 51 funded projects received additional time-limited funding for the second consecutive year to adapt components of their program activities to focus on children on the autism spectrum and their families living in OLMCs across Canada.
Approximately 22,000 participants (children aged 0 to 6 years and parents and guardians) benefited from the program and over 553 partnerships were established because of HEY program funding.
Survey data collected in 2021–22 (which is the most recent available) found that 77% of program participants gained knowledge and skills because of attending the program, 88% reported improved health behaviours and 73% reported that their health and well-being improved because of attending the program.

### Infectious Diseases and Climate Change Fund – Adapting to the Impacts of Climate Change
Type of transfer payment: Grant and Contribution Footnote 11
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018
Link to departmental result:
- Result 2.1: Infectious diseases are prevented and controlled
Link to the department's program inventory:
- Foodborne and Zoonotic Diseases
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Infectious Disease and Climate Change Fund (IDCCF) focuses on preparing and protecting Canadians from climate-sensitive infectious diseases that are zoonotic, foodborne and waterborne. This includes actions that stimulate public health innovation using a One Health approach by linking human, animal and environmental health and in advancing climate change adaptation.
The IDCCF addresses the impact of climate change on human health in Canada by:
- increasing capacity to understand and respond to the rising demands posed by climate-sensitive infectious diseases; and
- equipping health professionals and Canadians with timely and accurate information to better understand their risks and take measures to prevent infection.
The IDCCF focuses on the following monitoring and surveillance activities:
- building baseline data and enhancing knowledge and expertise to understand, predict and monitor current and future risks through innovative approaches to surveillance, detection and analysis of climate-sensitive infectious diseases; and
- developing collaborative and novel approaches for the collection, sharing and use of data to support evidence-informed public health actions that equip and empower Canadians to adapt.
The following activities focus on education and awareness:
- promoting the development, distribution and uptake of education and awareness materials for health professionals; and
- facilitating education, awareness and the dissemination of tools and best practices for Canadian communities and among population groups who are disproportionately impacted by climate change.
Since launching in 2017, the IDCCF has invested in 42 projects related to climate-sensitive infectious diseases in Canada that support surveillance and monitoring, education for health professionals, and public awareness activities. In 2024–25, the University of Winnipeg's Prairie Climate Centre launched a series of engaging activities for youth, promoting wellness for the earth through sustainability, as well as fostering a deeper understanding of climate issues.
These investments support activities to ensure Canadians and participants in the health system, including Indigenous communities, have the tools, information and knowledge needed to take measures to reduce the health effects of climate change. Investigation and analysis of knowledge gaps carried out through funded projects provide information on climate sensitive water-borne and zoonotic and vector-borne infectious diseases and facilitate new and expanded cross-sectoral collaboration and partnerships. Through funded projects, tools and training are developed, including enhanced resources for health professionals to improve their knowledge of health risks associated with climate-driven infectious diseases. This supports providers in responding more effectively to these diseases.

### Integrated Strategy for Healthy Living and Chronic Disease – Enhanced Surveillance for Chronic Disease
Type of transfer payment: Grant and Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates

Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.3: Chronic diseases are prevented
Link to the department's program inventory:

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program is to enhance capacity for public health chronic disease surveillance activities to expand data sources, address persistent public health surveillance evidence gaps and support the development of a robust evidence base on chronic diseases and conditions, injuries, problematic substance use and their risk factors in Canada.
Through the Enhanced Surveillance for Chronic Disease Program (ESCDP), 7 contribution agreements totalling $6.6 million over 3 fiscal years were signed in 2024. Projects funded through this initiative explored substance-related harms (including opioids), positive mental health and mental illness, and longer-term impacts of COVID-19. These projects explored the use of new technologies, tools and approaches to collect, use, and disseminate data for public health use.
Notable results included:
- The Canadian Center on Substance Use and Addiction (CCSA) is using artificial intelligence (AI) to examine data from social media, news websites, online images, drug alert sites, and other sources in order to enhance the real-time surveillance of substance-related harms. CCSA is also conducting a scoping review of the use of AI language models in healthcare to identify gaps and areas for improvement for the application of AI in addressing substance-related harms.
- The University of Toronto is developing a surveillance system to better understand the impact of the opioid crisis on maternal and child health at federal and provincial levels. This initiative also explores the intersection of opioid use and disability, chronic disease, and mental illness. Findings will contribute to the collaborative development of evidence-based policies, programs and services, clinical practice guidelines to support pregnant and/or postpartum people with opioid use and children with prenatal opioid exposure, and public health interventions.
- The British Columbia Centre for Disease Control is utilizing algorithms derived from health administrative data to create a surveillance system for ongoing monitoring of post COVID-19 condition (PCC) risk factors, healthcare utilization patterns, and health outcomes associated with PCC. This project is a collaborative effort among researchers and clinicians in British Columbia, Ontario, and Manitoba. By fostering cross-provincial information sharing among public health leaders, the project aims to optimize resource use, share knowledge, and promote best practices. This project could ultimately serve as a model for future collaborative, evidence-based healthcare initiatives.

### Integrated Strategy for Healthy Living and Chronic Disease – Pan-Canadian Joint Consortium for School Health
Type of transfer payment: Grant

- Result 1.2: Canadians have improved health behaviours
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Pan-Canadian Joint Consortium for School Health (JCSH) grant was established in 2005 to support the health, well-being, and achievement of children and youth in school settings, and brings together ministries responsible for health/wellness and education from most provinces and territories. The grant supports the JCSH Secretariat (through the Government of Prince Edward Island), which facilitates collaboration among provincial and territorial members to promote wellness and achievement in Canada's school-aged children and youth by way of a Health Promoting Schools approach. PHAC serves as the federal representative on the JCSH and acts in an advisor and observer capacity.
The School Health Grant for Youth (SHGY) program provides youth with the opportunity to apply for micro-grant funding to develop youth-led initiatives that encourage and promote healthy living in their school. Youth projects must align with the following PHAC priorities: substance use and related harms, positive mental health and well-being, healthy eating and nutrition, and physical activity. Funding for the SHGY comes from existing A-Base resources for the JCSH.
In 2024–25, the JCSH was in the final year of its four-year grant mandate awarded in April 2021. Grant funding from PHAC provided funding to support the operating costs of the JCSH Secretariat until 2025. The JCSH consists of the Secretariat and two committees: the School Health Coordinators Committee (SHCC), which consists of officials from participating provincial and territorial education and health/wellness ministries, and serves as a pan-Canadian forum to support collaboration and alignment between health/wellness and educator sectors in the promotion of student success and well-being, and the Management Committee, which consists of senior executive-level participants and provides the main forum for executive-level discussion and decisions affecting the work of the JCSH. With grant funding in place, the JCSH Secretariat convened several meetings of these committees, along with joint Management Committee-SHCC meetings and webinar events, which are an opportunity to hear from researchers, policy makers and other key players in the field of school-aged child and youth health, and to learn about latest research findings and initiatives.
The JCSH Secretariat organized, convened, and led seven Task Groups, whose objectives were to carry out the activities outlined in the grant mandate workplan. These Task Groups were as follows: JCSH Evaluation Response, Mandate Renewal, Youth Engagement Toolkit Revision, School Well-being Resource Revision, Healthy School Planner Revision, Reconciliation, and Research Network. In addition, the JCSH also shared knowledge and information through the distribution of numerous News Bundles and environmental scans from various jurisdictions. These activities contributed to information sharing, knowledge exchange, and capacity building among JCSH member jurisdictions.
Through the SHGY, PHAC awarded grant funding to 93 youth recipients in 2024–25 to develop and implement youth-led projects to support healthy living in schools. Participating youth gained knowledge, tools, and resources to support healthy living, and funding recipients gained valuable life skills such as leadership, project planning, and giving back to the community. Youth-led projects all focused on one of the grant's four priority areas, with the majority of youth projects focusing on positive mental health and well-being.
