Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The Intersectoral Action Fund (ISAF) aims to support action on the social determinants of health by strengthening capacity in communities to advance upstream intersectoral action in ways that improve population health, reduce health inequities, and strengthen community resiliency.
Following an open solicitation in 2024–25 which received strong community interest and a high number of applicants, ISAF started funding an additional 12 projects with $1.5 million in total funding that aim to bring health, equity, and well-being into local decision-making processes and drive meaningful change within their communities. ISAF also continued supporting 16 projects in communities across Canada. These projects are addressing a range of complex public health challenges, including housing; climate change; affording everyday essentials; and taking control of how food is grown, shared, and eaten.
Examples of results from projects receiving funding in 2024–25 include the following:
- The Council for Agencies Serving South Asians (CASSA) created the Racialized Health Working Group to drive systemic change in healthcare for racialized communities in Ontario. The Working Group was formed in collaboration with several key community-based organizations and engaged over 3,639 participants throughout their project via surveys, focus groups, and CASSA's Annual Health Equity Summit . Their research report titled " Bridging Health Divides: A collaborative study on addressing healthcare disparities in racialized communities " presented evidence-based recommendations to guide equitable policy changes and address ongoing health disparities across the province.
- Déclic , Initiatives pour la formation et l'emploi des jeunes (in French only) led a project based in Montreal, Quebec, which developed an intersectoral action protocol for former children and youth in care of Quebec's Director of Youth Protection. Youth who have aged out of care face numerous barriers to accessing education and employment. Existing adult education systems and structures are not designed to meet the needs of this population, who are often dealing with complex traumas. This protocol aims to promote multisectoral collaboration among stakeholders in the health, well-being, education, and employment sectors to better support the transition of former youth in care into adulthood.
ISAF is also supporting the Tamarack Institute for Community Engagement to help funding recipients develop knowledge and skills to better deliver their projects and to support knowledge sharing and best practices for project design and implementation.

The purpose of this project is to address significant knowledge gaps in mpox antiviral treatment through rapid response research, in collaboration with local public health authorities and priority populations disproportionately impacted by the outbreak. Improving understanding of mpox, treatment and the outbreak in Canada is essential to inform national and global response. This research is recommended by the WHO through a Research and Development Blueprint on mpox and an expert panel on mpox established by PHAC's Chief Public Health Officer and Chief Science Officer.
This project included two components:
- a feasibility study of an antiviral treatment in non-hospitalized patients with mpox infection (completed); and
- an observational cohort study to describe the clinical manifestations, psychosocial impacts, transmission dynamics, and virologic aspects of mpox infection.
Results for the feasibility study include analyses of clinical, quality of life, and virologic outcomes that are underway, and a report on the feasibility outcomes is still to be presented and published. Although the observational cohort study is ongoing, some early findings have been published and presented at scientific conferences. Funding for this study is complete.

### National Microbiology Laboratory – Integral Genomics Innovation Program
Type of transfer payment: Contribution
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2021–22
Link to departmental result:
- Result 2.2: Infectious disease outbreaks and threats are prepared for and responded to effectively
Link to the department's program inventory:
- Laboratory Science Leadership and Services
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this program is to strengthen public health infrastructure in Canada by helping public health laboratories to innovate and integrate genome-sequencing for the surveillance of public health pathogens. The program complements existing partnerships by accelerating the speed of genomic sequencing and integrating its outputs within existing public health surveillance activities.
This funding opportunity aimed to mobilize public health networks to enhance the generation and availability of genomic information for the detection and tracking of evolution within pathogens and public health threats (e.g., COVID-19, tuberculosis and antimicrobial resistance). The Integral Genomics Innovation Program aimed to improve the completeness and timely availability of genomic information on emerging variants and aimed to support PHAC's work with provinces, territories and international partners to improve monitoring, public health surveillance and outbreak response across Canada.
This funding opportunity supported the following program objectives:
- Accelerate the availability of analysis-ready genomics (sequences plus contextual data) within a province or territory;
- Improve the completeness, quality and maintainability of contextual data supporting genomics to better enable use of genomics in effective public health responses;
- Improve the bidirectional relationship between laboratory-generated sequences and health authority data to enable genomic epidemiology analyses (e.g., vaccine effectiveness by variant);
- Facilitate the timely dissemination, exchange and application of analysis-ready information with public health partners; and
- Contribute to a coherent and effective genomic surveillance network across Canada.
In 2024–25, the final year of its current funding cycle, the Integral Genomics Innovation Program, administered through PHAC, continued to support provincial and territorial public health laboratories, as well as academic institutions, in expanding and refining the use of genomics in public health surveillance. The program enabled funded partners to build on prior years' successes by further improving efficiency, data integration, and broadening the scope of surveillance for high consequence pathogens.
Laboratories funded through this program demonstrated increased capacity in their operations. For the first time, eight public health laboratories across Canada routinely sequenced both bacterial and viral pathogens, reflecting continued progress in the integration and expansion of genomic surveillance capabilities. Surveillance activities expanded in some laboratories to include additional pathogens such as tuberculosis (TB), sexually transmitted and blood-borne infections (STBBIs), influenza, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), and enteric pathogens.
Building on earlier efficiency gains, jurisdictions further optimized their sequencing pipelines. Turnaround times improved or held steady, with the Institut national de santé publique du Québec (INSPQ) notably reducing their turnaround from 28 days to 7-10 days in 2024–25.
The availability and completeness of critical contextual data also improved. Following year-over-year gains, several jurisdictions sustained or further advanced their progress in 2024–25. These improvements were supported by the continued adoption and scaling of tools for multi-pathogen use and contributed to data consistency both nationally and internationally.
Integral investments also supported progress in linking genomic and epidemiology data for more timely and integrated public health insights. In particular, the British Columbia Centre for Disease Control advanced the integration of laboratory, clinical, and wastewater data for real-time analysis. Public Health Ontario Laboratory launched a TB genomics program, to enhance connections between sequence data and case-level information, improving reporting workflows. Cadham Provincial Laboratory transitioned to cloud-based systems for HIV and STBBIs, supporting more streamlined data integration and enhanced surveillance.
A key marker of sustainability was reduced dependency on external sequencing services. In 2024–25, Newfoundland & Labrador Public Health Laboratory achieved operational self-sufficiency in whole genome sequencing surveillance, increasing local throughput and strengthening enhanced readiness for future surveillance demands.
Academic contributions furthered amplified progress for extending the relevance of Canadian public health genomic tools. Simon Fraser University, which had previously adapted data ontologies for COVID-19, expanded the model to additional pathogens, enabling broader application and international alignment.
By investing in locally-led innovation, the Integral Genomics Innovation Program helped accelerate national progress. Investments in 2024–25 not only advanced the integration of genomics into frontline public health workflows but also laid the groundwork for sustained capabilities. This program also reinforced genomics as a core public health tool and demonstrated transformation across a decentralized public health system by positioning jurisdictions to better respond to emerging challenges such as antimicrobial resistance, climate-sensitive pathogens, and the resurgence of vaccine-preventable diseases.

This initiative was examined as part of the Evaluation of the Corporate Data and Surveillance Modernization Activities completed in 2024–25.
Summary of Findings:
The Evaluation of the Corporate Data and Surveillance Modernization Activities found that the Integral Genomics Innovation Program supported public health laboratories in expanding their genomic sequencing and surveillance infrastructure, helped PT capacity in genomic sequencing to improve the quality and timeliness of data, and fostered stronger FPT relationships. This enabled PHAC to analyze data more quickly and identify potential outbreaks.

Engagement of applicants and recipients in 2024–25:
PHAC continued to engage closely with funded recipients through routine technical check-ins and collaborative activities designed to share lessons learned and align efforts nationally. In 2024–25, a second national summit brough together project stakeholders from eight provinces, along with their federal counterparts, to discuss progress and challenges in operationalising genomics for public health surveillance. The summit built on the success of the previous years and emphasized cross-jurisdictional learning. Key themes included the need for greater standardization in data formats and quality, improving equity by addressing barriers faced by populations in situations of vulnerability, advancing integration by focusing on workforce and processes (not just technology), and fostering innovation through locally driven solutions with national relevance. These engagements reinforced the value of the Integral program for collaborative problem-solving and continuous improvement across Canada's public health genomics landscape.

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program
The Contribution Agreement with McMaster University was established on January 27, 2023. The evidence-based guidelines for post COVID-19 condition (PCC) aim to develop, disseminate and evaluate six comprehensive, evidence-based guidelines on topics covering the full cycle of the condition. The objectives of the Post COVID-19 Condition (CAN-PCC) program include:
- Developing six evidence-based guidelines on PCC;
- Raising awareness about these guidelines and promote their uptake; and
- Ensuring effectiveness of these guidelines on the ultimate beneficiaries.
The Canadian Guidelines for Post COVID-19 Condition (CAN-PCC) have provided trustworthy, evidence-informed guidelines, comprised of over 100 recommendations, to support individuals with PCC, as well as health professionals and policymakers. Over 150 vetted experts contributed to this work, supported by a conflict-of-interest framework and inclusive governance structure. The development process was shaped by extensive engagement with persons with lived experience, health professionals, public health leaders, and equity-deserving populations, ensuring the guidelines reflect diverse perspectives and health equity considerations. In addition to the guidelines, 151 knowledge mobilization products, available in 15 languages, were produced to support uptake and accessibility.
This collaborative, equity-oriented approach enabled the creation of practical, user-friendly guidance that supports more equitable care and better health outcomes for Canadians affected by PCC.
All guidelines and relating materials are publicly available at canpcc.ca .

### Strengthening the Baby-Friendly Initiative
Type of transfer payment: Grant
This grant was funded as one of four priorities of the President and Chief Public Health Officer that could be advanced through short-term investments using surplus funds in the Agency.
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
The purpose of this grant is to promote maternal and infant health in Canada by strengthening Canada's implementation of the Baby-Friendly Initiative, an evidence-based global initiative that has proven to be effective for increasing breastfeeding rates.
The objectives are to:
- Modernize the Baby-Friendly Initiative in Canada to reflect updated guidance from the World Health Organization and input from Canadian stakeholders;
- Adapt, test and evaluate a national quality improvement initiative to develop a model to support facilities in achieving the "Baby-Friendly" designation; and
- Raise awareness of the Baby-Friendly Initiative in Canada.
An evaluation of the National Baby-Friendly Initiative Quality Improvement Collaborative Project was completed in 2022 and found significant improvements consistently across Baby-Friendly Initiative (BFI) indicators. These included:
- 118% improvement in the proportion of families receiving assistance and support for breastfeeding within six hours of birth;
- 90.4% improvement in rooming in (mother/babies staying together);
- 82.8% improvement in skin to skin within five minutes of caesarean birth;
- 73.3% improvement in skin to skin for at least an hour after caesarean births; and
- 46.1% improvement in practicing skin to skin for at least an hour after vaginal births.
The evaluation also found improvement in critical success factors that supported the ability to advance BFI goals. These included improvements in teamwork, commitment of leadership, the ability to collect and analyze data, parent partner voices, sharing and learning with other facilities across the country, and support from the Breastfeeding Committee for Canada (BCC). The achievements occurred against a backdrop of a global pandemic that diverted resources in all facilities to support the COVID-19 pandemic response. The evaluation results have been presented internationally.
A Coach Mentor Program was launched in 2022 to build on and scale up the impacts achieved through the project. Through this program, participants in the initial BFI project were trained to support a new cohort of hospitals and community health facilities towards becoming BFI designated. A second phase of the Coach Mentor Program took place through 2024–25.
A communications strategy was developed and implemented to raise awareness of the Baby-Friendly Initiative and build understanding of evidence based best-practices to promote, protect, and support breastfeeding.
In 2023–24 and 2024–25, additional funding was provided to increase the scope and impact of the work undertaken through the grant. This supported two national workshops, one aimed to build capacity for scaling up modernized tools for implementation of the BFI, while the other workshop engaged Indigenous health leaders to inform culturally safe strategies to scale up BFI and improve breastfeeding rates in Indigenous communities. In March 2025, a two-day engagement event brought together senior health leaders from across Canada to explore key barriers and challenges to BFI implementation and designation, with the goal of developing innovative, actionable strategies to overcome these obstacles and accelerate progress.

### Type 2 Diabetes Prevention Challenge
Type of transfer payment: Grant

Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
In partnership with Impact Canada , PHAC is delivering the Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) Prevention Challenge to support innovators in developing and implementing community co-designed approaches that address the barriers and determinants of health that lead to an increased risk for T2D for communities in Canada.
This challenge is delivered through a Grand Challenge model, which uses open and thematic competitions to fund a broad range of potential innovations on a prospective basis and focuses on rigorous evaluations of effectiveness. Delivery will be in three stages:
- Stage 1: Challenge Launch and Proposed Concept Application Intake;
- Stage 2: Concept Development; and
- Stage 3: Implementation and Evidence Generation.
In July 2024, the seven finalists of Stage 3 of the Type 2 Diabetes Challenge were announced. Each finalist received a $600,000 prize and began to test and evaluate the effectiveness of their concept, as it is being implemented. The finalists are in the process of generating evidence that their approach reduces the number of individuals who are at risk to develop Type 2 Diabetes in their community using their method of understanding and measuring that risk.

### Youth Substance Use Prevention Program (Renewed Canadian Drugs and Substances Strategy)
Type of transfer payment: Grant and Contribution Footnote 14
Type of appropriation: Appropriated annually through Estimates
Fiscal year for terms and conditions: 2018–19
Link to departmental result:
- Result 1.1: Canadians have improved physical and mental health
Link to the department's program inventory:
Purpose and objectives of transfer payment program:
In the 2023 federal budget, the Government of Canada announced an investment of $20.2 million for a new, community-based program to prevent substance use and its related harms among young people, as part of the renewed Canadian Drugs and Substances Strategy. Health Canada is the federal lead for this horizontal initiative. Using a collaborative approach, the Youth Substance Use Prevention Program focuses on building protective factors that promote overall health and well-being, and prevent substance use harms among youth. These factors include healthy relationships, safe environments and school and community connectedness.
The Program is based on the Icelandic Prevention Model (IPM) which is recognized internationally for its collaborative approach to preventing substance use harms among youth. Developed by the Icelandic Centre for Social Research and Analysis (ICSRA), it applies a community-driven approach to influence the risk and protective factors associated with substance use. Since it originated in 1997, ICSRA has expanded their work to over 30 countries worldwide under the organization name of Planet Youth.
The Program aims to build capacity in communities to implement an upstream youth substance use prevention model that follows the guiding principles and key steps of the IPM, support the Canadian implementation of the IPM, and support the subsequent knowledge development and exchange based on the evaluation of the IPM in the Canadian context.
In 2024–25, the Public Health Agency of Canada supported 12 projects in Nova Scotia, Ontario, Saskatchewan, Alberta, and British Columbia.
Results will be updated following the completion of the first YSUPP reporting cycle.
